data.title: Crane NXT: Shares Offer Strong Potential On Continued Growth
data.description:
May 8th ended up being a really good day for shareholders of Crane NXT (NYSE:CXT), a company that serves as the only supplier of US paper currency to the US government, as well as a provider of certain payment innovations such as those involving the detection and authentication of payments. After reporting financial results for the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year after the market closed on May 7th, shares of the company spiked on May 8th, closing up 7%. Even with this move higher, the stock is down quite a bit since I last reaffirmed it as a ‘strong buy’ in February of this year. During this time, shares have fallen by 17.4%. This is far worse than the 6% drop experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. In all likelihood, investors are worried about the effects that a difficult economy could have on the business and, perhaps, the exposure of the business has to the US government and potential cost-cutting initiatives implemented by the Trump Administration.

Despite these uncertainties, I remain optimistic about Crane NXT. The stock looks attractively priced on an absolute basis to me. Shares might be a bit mixed compared to other similar financial firms. But management seems optimistic about the long-term trajectory of the company. Management continues to make acquisitions aimed at growing the business. And if it can achieve its goal of hitting $3 billion in annual revenue by 2028, the upside for shareholders could be massive. Because of all of this, I have no problem keeping the company rated a ‘strong buy’ for now.

Shares are still worth the cash

The first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a pretty good time for Crane NXT from a fundamental perspective compared to what analysts were anticipating. Take revenue as an example. During the quarter, the company generated sales of $330.3 million. That's an increase of 5.3% compared to the $313.6 million the business reported one year earlier. The revenue reported by the company ended up being $11.8 million above what analysts were hoping to see. This rise in revenue came in spite of the fact that sales associated with its Crane Payment Innovations segment contracted from $209 million to $202.9 million.

According to management, while service revenue grew by 1.2% because of higher pricing on the services it provides, payment acceptance and dispensing products revenue dropped 3.7%. The company chalked this up to lower gaming volumes at more than offset higher pricing. Foreign currency fluctuations also negatively affected revenue $2.4 million, with most of that being attributable to the weakening of the Japanese yen compared to the US dollar.

The real growth engine for the company during the quarter was its Security and Authentication Technologies segment. Revenue jumped 21.8% from $104.6 million to $127.4 million. This was in spite of the fact that the revenue associated with banknotes and security products actually fell 9.7% from $103.4 million to $93.4 million. Management blamed this on a decrease in core sales of 7.4%. However, this was expected to be the case. I say this because the weakening came from lower sales to the US government because of planned shutdowns of certain paper making equipment as the US government prepares for new banknote series that are coming out.

This was something I detailed as a growth catalyst for the company when I last wrote about it earlier this year. In 2026, the US government is coming out with a new $10 bill. This would be followed up in 2028 with a new $50 bill. A new $20 bill is slated to come out in 2030, while a new $5 bill should be released in 2032. All of these are exciting. But, to me, the most exciting will be the new $100 bill that is coming out in 2034. That's because this will be the first instance of US currency having micro-optics included in it. Management was very clear that 2025 would be a transition year as we move in the direction of these new banknote issuances. In fact, the firm forecasted a 20% volume decline this year alone because of these activities. Seeing as how Crane NXT has been the only supplier of US paper currency to the US government since 1879, I believe that it stands to benefit significantly from this transition.

Even though that revenue dropped, the company saw authentication products and solutions revenue skyrocket from $1.2 million to $34 million. This was largely because of the firm's decision to acquire OpSec Security, an authentication solutions and brand and digital content protection company that was purchased for $270 million in May of last year. As a result of this, revenue associated with this segment managed to rise, and because last year was only a partial year regarding its ownership of OpSec Security, I fully expect that business to be the gift that keeps on giving this year.

This wasn't the only acquisition that the company has focused on over the last year or so. On May 1st of this year, the company announced that it had acquired De La Rue Authentication, a provider of digital and physical security and authentication technologies, in a transaction valued at £300 million. This is all part of the plan that the business has to grow its revenue to $3 billion by 2028. That would be more than double the $1.49 billion generated last year. At that time, the company expects to have an EBITDA margin that's in the high 20% range. If we construe this to mean 27.5%, it would imply EBITDA of $825 million. That compares to the $396.7 million, or 26.7% EBITDA margin, that the company had for 2024.

For the first quarter of 2025, Crane NXT did report earnings per share of only $0.38. That was down from the $0.66 reported the previous year and fell short of analysts’ expectations by $0.03. A lot of this weakness was because of one time items, such as those involving its acquisitions. But even on an adjusted basis, there was a decline in earnings per share from $0.85 to $0.54. The good thing here is that adjusted earnings per share ended up being $0.03 greater than what analysts were hoping to see. Unfortunately, this still meant a drop in adjusted net income from $49.2 million to $31.3 million. At least that was better than the decline in GAAP net profits from $55.4 million to $21.7 million. Other profitability metrics worsened as well. Operating cash flow, for instance, fell from $9.5 million to negative $19.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $60.3 million to $42.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company contracted from $80.7 million to $61.1 million.

In addition to coming in higher than what analysts were hoping for when it came to revenue and adjusted earnings per share, the company also increased guidance for 2025. They now expect revenue to grow by between 6% and 8%. That compares to the 1% to 3% rise previously forecasted. This is largely because of the aforementioned acquisition of De La Rue Authentication. Earnings per share, however, are forecasted to still remain between $4.00 and $4.30. This stagnant expectation when it comes to earnings per share can likely be attributed to the fact that management is now forecasting some impact from tariffs. So long as Mexico remains exempt under the USMCA, the firm anticipates an impact of $25 million to its cost of goods sold. This is because of CPI hardware products that largely come from China. Management did say that this impact could cost the business another $15 million if tariffs on Mexico come into play. The firm did say that it implemented price increases to offset a majority of the current tariffs. But it appears as though they will be eating some of it.

With that in mind, if we assume that the profitability metrics for the company are as projected, then in the chart above, you can see how shares are currently priced on a forward basis. This chart also values the company based on historical results from 2024. This is a business that looks attractively priced, but would not normally be a ‘strong buy’ in my book. That is especially true when you consider how the stock is priced compared to the five similar firms I stacked it up against in the table below. Yes, on a price to earnings basis, Crane NXT is the cheapest of the group. But on a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the five companies are cheaper than it is. This number rises to four of the five when using the EV to EBITDA approach.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Crane NXT 12.0 11.2 9.0
Global Payments (GPN) 12.8 5.6 7.8
WEX Inc (WEX) 16.3 33.6 5.4
Fidelity National Information Services (FISI) 51.3 17.5 14.4
The Brink's Co. (BCO) 25.7 9.8 8.2
Payoneer Global (PAYO) 19.8 13.7 8.8

Even though the stock might not be the cheapest thing out there, if we believe that management is right about its objectives, and can achieve them, then upside could be quite significant. In the chart below, I projected out how much upside shares my experience if the company can hit its target for 2028. This chart shows the annualized upside that shares would enjoy if the firm trades at EV to EBITDA multiples of between 9 and 15, with the lower end of that range exactly where the stock is trading at the moment. I would argue that shares probably deserve to trade at a slightly higher multiple than where they are right now. So I think annualized upside of 20% or higher is wholly achievable. It is worth noting that this all assumes that the net leverage ratio of the company is under 3 by the end of this forecast period. This would be in alignment with management's prior view that the net leverage ratio would be beneath that number. I have settled on a reading of exactly 2.99. Obviously, if the net leverage ratio ends up being lower, upside would be even more significant. As an example, if we assume a reading of 2.5, then annualized upside would be 17.6% using the EV to EBITDA multiple of 9.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that the market is wrong about Crane NXT. The company seems to be a good quality business that is growth oriented. Shares might not be the cheapest, but the firm's growth plans are promising. It looks like tariffs and broader economic concerns could prove to be a bit of a risk factor for the company. But I don't think that those are enough to justify the downside that shares have experienced as of late. At the end of the day, I believe that maintaining the business as a ‘strong buy’ candidate makes sense.

data.title: Uber: Accelerates AV Ambitions As Growth Shows Signs Of Stalling
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Uber (NYSE:UBER)(NEOE:UBER:CA), I analysed the company’s Q4 results and examined why there was a lot of negativity in the name post the earnings release. I also investigated whether the AV threat that investors have kept using against the name was real or merely an overreaction. I upgraded my rating to a BUY on the stock with a price target of $103.

Since the article was published in February 2025, UBER’s stock has surged, gaining 18.6% and significantly outperforming the S&P 500, which declined 6.4% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the company’s Q1 report and examine how the company has been navigating the tricky macro-environment. I also look at whether there have been any new developments on the AV front, especially on the back of reports of Tesla's (TSLA) imminent entry into the space.

A Snapshot of Q1 Earnings Report

UBER had a mixed start to the year, with a bottom-line beat and a top-line miss. More specifically, Q1 revenues came in at $11.53 billion, up 13.8% y/y but missing analyst estimates by $86.25 million. Diluted EPS, on the other hand, came in at $0.83, comfortably beating analyst estimates by $0.33. Q1 gross bookings grew 18% y/y on a constant currency basis, coming in at $42.8 billion. Adjusted EBITDA of $1.87 billion was up 35% y/y and beat analyst estimates of $1.84 billion. The company generated operating cash flows of $2.3 billion, ending the quarter with $6 billion in unrestricted cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments.

For the second quarter of FY25, the company expects gross bookings to come in between $45.75 billion and $47.25 billion, which includes an approximate 1.5% current headwind. The midpoint of the outlook, $46.5 billion, came ahead of analyst estimates of $45.9 billion. Adjusted EBITDA is now expected to come in between $2.02 billion and $2.12 billion. The midpoint of the EBITDA outlook, $2.07 billion, came in marginally ahead of the analyst estimate of $2.05 billion.

Decline in Gross Bookings Concerning but Not Alarming

If one looks at UBER’s Q1 numbers, then one of the first major takeaways would be the sharp deceleration in the growth rate of gross bookings, with Mobility being the culprit. More specifically, from a 17.6% y/y growth in Q4, Q1 saw total gross bookings’ growth rate sharply decline to 13.7% y/y. While both mobility and delivery gross bookings both registered a decline in growth, the former had a steeper decline, going from 18.2% in Q4 to 13.5% in Q1.

What is slightly concerning to me as an UBER investor is that the decline in growth is not restricted to Q1 alone. On the contrary, there has been a steady pattern of decline in recent quarters, with Q4 being the only exception, as evidenced by the charts below.

If one looks at adjusted EBITDA over the more recent quarters, then the trend is the same as that of gross bookings.

UBER’s management was asked about this trend during the earnings call, and they attributed the decline in gross bookings’ growth to a higher growth in trips internationally relative to the US, which affected the overall price mix, subsequently affecting the gross bookings’ growth. While this makes sense, investors, in my opinion, should keep a close eye on this trend going forward, to see if the situation improves, especially with respect to adjusted EBITDA. UBER’s management, during the last Investor Day, had projected adjusted EBITDA to grow at a high 30s to 40% CAGR over the next three years. While there is no threat to this target today, should this downward trend continue, then it would certainly be a cause for worry.

While I find these trends to be concerning, I don’t find them alarming yet for multiple reasons. Firstly, such a trend has occurred in the past. More specifically, the gross bookings’ growth rate declined from the second quarter of FY22 to the second quarter of FY23 before picking up again. As for adjusted EBITDA, while the growth rate has decelerated, one has to keep in mind that adjusted EBITDA was doubling and tripling in FY22 and FY23. Such a growth rate is not feasible, and one could view the current trend as stability setting in. Therefore, while investors should keep a close eye on the growth trend, there is no reason for them to panic yet in my opinion.

Secondly, if one looks at UBER’s other key metrics, there is a lot to cheer. For instance, monthly active platform consumers (MAPCs) are growing at a steady rate with each passing quarter. And adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of gross bookings is also growing at a steady rate, which demonstrates how the company has become more disciplined with each passing quarter. Finally, UBER continues to be a free cash flow generating machine. Q1 was no different, with operating cash flows registering a y/y growth of 64% and free cash flows registering a y/y growth of 66%.

Overall, when I look at UBER holistically, while there are certainly areas of concern, overall, the business continues to be on a solid footing. Having said that, given the challenging macro-environment that the company finds itself in today, it is vital that Dara & Co. continue to adopt a disciplined approach and ensure that they stem the decline in the growth rates, especially with respect to adjusted EBITDA.

UBER’s AV Ambitions Remain on Track

The other key takeaway from UBER’s Q1 report is the progress made by the ride-hailing giant when it comes to autonomous vehicles (AVs). Management announced that the partnership with Waymo in Austin was progressing well, with healthy opt-in rates and ratings. The company also announced that there was “very high utilization” of the AVs in terms of daily trips, with the “average Waymo in Austin busier than 99% of Austin drivers,” in terms of the number of trips per day per Waymo.

Moreover, the company also announced a new AV partnership with Pony AI (PONY) under which robotaxis will be launched in the Middle East later this year. UBER also announced earlier this week that it has expanded its partnership with WeRide (WRD), through which AVs will be rolled out in 15 additional cities globally over the next five years, including in Europe.

In my last article on UBER, I mentioned how the market’s “obsession” over the notion of autonomous vehicles being a serious threat was false. I also argued how UBER had the potential to be a ready-made partner for AV companies. Q1 proved this to be the case and I would expect more partnerships in the coming quarters. TSLA’s entry into the robotaxi space is not going to change that.

Valuation

Forward EV/EBITDA Multiple Approach

Price Target

$89.00

Projected Forward EV/EBITDA multiple

21.5x

Projected FY25 Adjusted EBITDA

$8.70 billion

Cash & Cash Equivalents

$6.03 billion

Long-Term Debt

$9.58 billion

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), Uber’s Q1FY25 Press Release, and Author’s Calculations

In my last article on UBER, I had projected FY25 gross bookings to come in at $192.1 billion, which represents a y/y growth of 18%. As mentioned earlier, the y/y growth rate in gross bookings has been steadily declining, with the growth rate of gross bookings in Q1, in particular, showing a sharp deceleration. The company, as mentioned earlier, expects Q2 gross bookings to come in between $45.75 billion and $47.25 billion. The midpoint, $46.5 billion would represent a y/y growth of 16.4%, better than the 13.7% y/y growth it registered in the 1st quarter. Moreover, in Q1, the gross bookings came in at the midpoint of the company’s guidance. If one takes the last five quarters, the company, on average, has registered a y/y growth of 17.2%. So, I have assumed Q2 gross bookings growth rate at 16.4%, and a growth rate of 17.2% for Q3 and Q4. At these growth rates, Q2 gross bookings would come in at $46.5 billion, Q3 gross bookings would come in at $48.01 billion, and Q4 gross bookings would come in at $51.8 billion. Thus, total gross bookings for FY25 are projected to come in at $189.1 billion, marginally lower than my previous estimate.

The company now expects adjusted EBITDA for the second quarter to come in between $2.02 billion and $2.12 billion. The midpoint of this estimate, $2.07 billion, would represent a y/y growth of 31.8% and represents 4.5% of the gross bookings. If one looks at UBER’s past few quarters, the adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of gross bookings has been sequentially going up, on average by 0.2%. I have assumed this magnitude of increase for Q3 and Q4. This would mean Q3 adjusted EBITDA would be 4.7% of gross bookings and Q4 adjusted EBITDA would be 4.9% of gross bookings. This implies that Q3 adjusted EBITDA is projected to come in at $2.26 billion and Q4 adjusted EBITDA is projected to come in at $2.54 billion. Thus, total adjusted EBITDA for FY25 is now projected to come in at $8.7 billion, lower than my previous estimate of $9.08 billion.

Based on LSEG Data, UBER currently trades at a forward EV/adjusted EBITDA of 17.8x, well below its long-term median of 21.5x. Despite the strong growth seen in adjusted EBITDA, given my lower projections of adjusted EBITDA, I have lowered my multiple of UBER from my previous estimate of 23.9x to the historical multiple of 21.5x. At this multiple, the company’s Enterprise Value is projected to be $187.05 billion.

After adding cash and cash equivalents of $6.03 billion and subtracting the long-term debt of $9.58 billion, the total equity value of Uber comes to $183.5 billion. The company has 2.08 billion shares in free float, which results in a price target of $89, lower than my previous price target of $103, but still represents an upside of about 6.4% from current levels.

UBER’s stock has very strong momentum, as evidenced by an overall grade of A on Seeking Alpha’s momentum scale. The short-term momentum also has an A grade. As such, despite the limited upside, I am still maintaining my BUY rating on the stock.

I have two caveats, however, to my BUY rating. Firstly, I won’t be adding any shares to my existing position because UBER is already my biggest position in my portfolio. And secondly, my current rating is predominantly based on the strong momentum and on my belief that UBER remains a “buy-on-any-weakness name”, given its market position and its long-term growth potential.

Risk Factors

In addition to the decelerating growth in both gross bookings and adjusted EBITDA, I would also factor in the imminent arrival of TSLA’s robotaxi in Austin. One can argue that UBER would be shielded due to its partnership with Waymo, which has had a head start in Austin. However, long-term investors should factor in the competition from TSLA as a potential headwind to UBER’s AV ambitions, especially if Mr. Musk refuses to partner with the ride-hailing giant.

The other risk factor is that its main competitor in the US, Lyft (LYFT), could still become an acquisition target, especially for those players with AV ambitions. Earlier this year, in January, there were reports about AMZN interested in acquiring LYFT. While nothing materialized then, it doesn’t mean that an acquisition cannot happen down the road, especially as the AV race heats up. Back in January, LYFT had a market cap of about $4.5 billion. Today, its market cap stands at $5.3 billion, which is not significantly higher. Should such an acquisition materialize, especially by a company like AMZN, then UBER could face formidable competition in the US. While not a significant risk factor today, it is a point that investors should factor in, especially from a long-term perspective.

Concluding Thoughts

UBER had a mixed start to the year, with a miss on the top-line but a beat on the bottom-line. The company also generated strong free cash flows. While both gross bookings and adjusted EBITDA generated strong double-digit growth, the growth rates of both the metrics have been showing a pattern of steady decline in recent quarters. While not a cause for alarm yet, investors should certainly keep a close eye on these metrics in the coming quarters, especially since the macro-environment has become challenging.

The recent quarter also saw UBER’s AV ambitions progress to the next level. Its partnership with Waymo in Austin is progressing well, and the company has brought the total number of partnerships with AV players to 18, with PONY becoming the latest partner. Any talk of AVs being a threat to UBER is becoming more farcical with each passing quarter.

From a valuation perspective, there is a limited upside at current levels. However, the stock has excellent momentum. As such, while I am maintaining my BUY rating on the stock, it is more of a “buy-on-any-weakness” call rather than a “conviction buy.”

UBER is finally getting the recognition it deserves, as evidenced by the 38.1% jump in the stock YTD. However, based on the moves it is making in the AV space, this is not a company that is resting on its laurels.

data.title: The Next Frontier In Portfolio Design: Keeping Equities While Adding Gold
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA | Jeremy Schwartz, CFA

Gold and stocks serve fundamentally different roles in a portfolio, and history makes their contrast crystal clear.

Over the past two centuries, gold has been a relatively stable store of purchasing power, offering real returns of just 0.8% per year—barely ahead of inflation. It's inert by design: it yields nothing, it doesn't grow, and it doesn't compound. Its primary function is wealth preservation, especially in times of political upheaval, monetary disorder or inflation shocks. In contrast, stocks represent dynamic claims on real assets—productive enterprises with reinvestment, growth and innovation embedded in their structure. From 1802 to April 2025, a diversified equity portfolio delivered real returns of nearly 7% annually, compounding purchasing power exponentially over generations.

The true edge of stocks lies in time and reinvestment.

A dollar invested in equities in 1802 would have grown to more than $2.4 million in real terms by April 2025; the same dollar in gold would be worth just $6.38.

In figure 1:

  • "Real" refers to "real return," which is indicating these are returns above the rate of inflation. A positive real return is indicating that it is increasing the purchasing power for the holder, whereas a negative real return is indicating a decline in purchasing power.
  • "Bonds" and "Bills" refer to U.S. government securities. Bills typically have maturities below one year, whereas in U.S. government securities, the term "bond" tends to refer to maturities of 10 years and beyond.

Figure 1: Looking at More than 200 Years of Market Data

These disparities grow even more striking when reinvested dividends and corporate earnings growth are considered. Despite all the crashes, wars and crises, equities have persistently outpaced not just gold, but every other asset class in terms of long-run performance. And importantly, stocks become less risky than bonds or gold over long holding periods—contrary to conventional wisdom. Siegel's data demonstrates that over any 20-year period, stocks have always maintained purchasing power and often delivered positive real returns even when other assets faltered.

Yet, 2025 is adding an intriguing twist to the historical script.

Gold has surged in popularity amid mounting geopolitical tensions, rising central bank purchases (especially by BRICS1 nations) and a market increasingly wary of fiat currency degradation. With inflation having surprised to the upside in several economies, and the prospect of structurally higher government debt levels, investors are revisiting gold not merely as a hedge but as a speculative asset with short-run return potential. This is a break from its long-standing role—people aren't just hedging, they're chasing. That's not unprecedented (e.g., the 1970s), but it's rare.

Some may see that reference to the 1970s and recall that the U.S. dollar was backed by gold until 1971 when President Nixon broke this link. We can measure the performance of the different assets in figure 1 from this specific point to see what has transpired. Figure 2 shows:

  • The cumulative real return of the U.S. dollar indicated an 87% decline.
  • Gold's real return was second only to stocks, and it's notable that this period included a very positive bond market environment, where interest rates declined from double-digit levels as recently as the early 1980s.

Figure 2: Asset Class Real Returns Since the End of the Gold Standard in 1971

This cyclical interest in gold may offer tactical opportunities, but structurally, the long game still favors equities.

Investors must weigh the current enthusiasm for gold against its long-run limitations. Stocks, by contrast, continue to benefit from reinvested earnings, global innovation cycles and a compounding engine gold simply doesn't possess. In that sense, gold may shine temporarily in 2025's macro fog, but equities remain the vehicle most likely to deliver lasting wealth creation across decades.

When evaluating gold in the context of long-term portfolio allocation, the fundamental challenge is not with gold itself, but with what must be sacrificed to hold it.

Gold, historically, preserves purchasing power and offers protection in certain macro environments. Yet, it offers no earnings, dividends or internal compounding engine. Consequently, funding a gold allocation usually requires reducing exposure to other assets—most notably equities. Given Professor Siegel's extensive data, which shows equities compounding real wealth at approximately 6.8% per year over two centuries, diverting capital from equities carries an opportunity cost. If the long-term outperformance of equities persists, as history overwhelmingly suggests it might, then allocating too heavily to gold risks meaningfully lower long-run portfolio growth.

This framing doesn't argue that gold allocations are unwise—it simply clarifies the trade-off.

Gold may shine in certain environments: monetary debasement, geopolitical upheaval, inflation spikes. In the short run, these can justify tactical gold exposures. However, the key question for the strategic, long-term investor is what gets displaced. Historically, taking dollars away from equities—arguably the highest real-returning asset class ever recorded—has been costly when measured over decades. Thus, for investors deeply focused on long-horizon wealth creation, the real risk is not holding gold per se, but reducing compounding equity exposure during periods when stocks continue their historical dominance.

However, modern portfolio construction tools, such as WisdomTree's capital-efficient strategies, offer a potential solution to this dilemma.

Rather than funding a gold position directly by selling equities, investors can use capital-efficient exposures that embed derivatives or alternative structuring techniques to maintain effective equity market participation while layering in additional assets like gold. In this way, investors can achieve additive exposure to gold without necessarily diluting their equity beta. This approach does not eliminate uncertainty—future returns on any asset class are always unknowable—but it does mitigate the opportunity cost risk associated with mechanically reallocating from equities into gold.

Viewed through the lens of Siegel's historical research, this becomes a subtle but important innovation.

It enables investors to engage with the tactical case for gold (especially relevant in 2025's volatile macro environment) while still honoring the structural advantage of equities over the long term. In short, if Siegel's framework remains directionally correct—that equities are the engine of long-term real wealth—then capital-efficient strategies could allow investors to "have their cake and eat it too": participating in potential short-run gold upside while minimizing the long-run compounding penalty of stepping away from equities.

We thought it could be useful to explore how an allocation to gold could impact a portfolio of U.S. assets, starting at 60% equities and 40% fixed income.

Introducing the ETF Toolkit

  • WisdomTree U.S. Efficient Core Fund (NTSX): The exposure is defined as 90% invested in the 500 largest U.S. equities, defined by market capitalization, with the other 10% in short-term U.S. Treasuries as collateral for a futures position. The futures position is defined as a 60% notional exposure to U.S. Treasury futures. The total exposure, therefore, includes the impact of leverage, as 90% exposure to equities + 60% exposure to U.S. Treasury futures = 150% combined exposure, or, put another way, every $100 invested leads to a notional exposure of $150. Leverage has the potential to increase the volatility of an investment relative to either of the underlying asset classes viewed individually.
  • WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund (GDE): The exposure is defined as 90% invested in the 500 largest U.S. equities, defined by market capitalization, with the other 10% in short-term U.S. Treasuries as collateral for a futures position. The futures position is defined as a 90% notional exposure to gold futures. The total exposure, therefore, includes the impact of leverage, as 90% exposure to equities + 90% exposure to gold futures = 180% combined exposure, or, put another way, every $100 is exposed to $180 notionally of equities and gold futures, split equally between the two. Leverage has the potential to increase the volatility of an investment relative to either of the underlying asset classes viewed individually. GDE represents a "long" position in equities and a long position in gold futures, thereby benefiting if these asset classes are delivering positive returns.
  • iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV): IVV seeks to track the price and yield performance of an index, the S&P 500, that is comprised of large market capitalization U.S. companies.
  • iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG): AGG seeks to track the price and yield performance of an index, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, comprised of certain segments of investment-grade U.S. fixed income.
  • SPDR Gold Shares (GLD): The investment objective of the SPDR® Gold Trust (the "Trust") is for SPDR® Gold Shares ("GLD") to reflect the performance of the price of gold bullion, less the Trust's expenses.

Determining the Allocations

Looking at figure 3, we can see that Blending Gold (50/40/10) is taking the traditional approach. GLD at 10% represents one-fifth the relative exposure to U.S. equities, with IVV at 50%.

Within Capital Efficient Gold Blend, we are using two capital-efficient ETF allocation tools. Allocating 83.3% to NTSX leads us to a U.S. equity starting point of 75%. If we then allocate 16.7% to GDE, we get a 15% overall gold exposure. Fifteen percent is one-fifth of the 75%.

But Capital Efficient Gold Blend does not stop there. GDE also generates 15% exposure to U.S. equities, bringing the total U.S. equity exposure to 90%. If one had placed 100% of the allocation into NTSX, by design, this would also generate 90% exposure to U.S. equities. Therefore, it is like we added GDE and did not take anything away from U.S. equities. Of course, capital efficient exposure is not a "free lunch," in the sense that using leverage in this way can increase volatility. To think about risk, the worst case would be if U.S. equities, U.S. fixed income and gold are all going down during the same time frame. Historical analysis indicates that this has not happened often on a calendar year basis, but 2022 would be the most recent such year.

Figure 3: Introducing the Two Approaches to Adding Gold to an Allocation

Figure 4 shows the standardized period returns of the different blends as well as the different ETFs that are used to create them.

Figure 4: Standardized Performance

2023 & 2024: Two Strong U.S. Equity Years

In figure 5, due to the available history of GDE, we can look at two full calendar years.

These years were characterized by strong, upwardly trending U.S. equities, driven largely by the largest companies. The Standard 60/40 delivered 18% in 2023 and 15.5% in 2024. The returns of Blending Gold (50/40/10), which basically took 10% from IVV and placed it in GLD, were very similar.

The Capital Efficient Gold Blend notably outperformed. This is because the capital-efficient approach did not allocate to gold at the expense of equities. Many might note, correctly, that this is just two years, but these two years do illustrate the behavior of these blends in the face of at least one important risk: the risk of allocating to gold before a strong upwardly trending U.S. equity market.

Figure 5: Minimizing the Opportunity Cost of Gold vs. U.S. Equities

2025 Represented a Shift in U.S. Equity Market Leadership

The first four months of 2025 will not be remembered for a smooth, upward-trending path of U.S. equity performance.

  • During April 2025 (Quarter-to-Date) we see that the Capital Efficient Gold Blend outperformed the Standard 60/40 to the downside, as it also did during the Year-to-Date timeframe.
  • The 3-Year figure is also notable because it includes nine months of 2022, which was a notably tough year where U.S. equities, U.S. fixed income and gold did not deliver strong returns.

Figure 6: Navigating a Few Shifting Market Environments

An Analysis of Rolling Periods

When we pulled up the rolling 12-month periods (limited due to GDE having just more than three years of total live history), we saw something surprising.

  • The Standard 60/40, on a rolling 12-month basis, did not look very different from Blending Gold (50/40/10). This is telling us that taking 10% away from IVV and placing it in GLD, on a rolling 12-month return basis, was largely indistinguishable than simply sticking with the Standard 60/40 exposure.
  • The Capital Efficient Gold Blend did not outperform in every period, but when it did, it added a noticeable 12-month rolling return margin. The periods of underperformance were reasonably close to the returns of the Standard 60/40.

Figure 7: The Surprise of the Rolling 12-Month Return Comparison

Conclusion

At its core, the debate about gold versus equities is a test of how investors think about time, risk and opportunity. Gold shines when fear dominates, but equities build enduring wealth when patience prevails—a truth reinforced across centuries of market history and crisply articulated in Stocks for the Long Run. The real risk with gold isn't that it fails; it's that by funding it through reduced equity exposure, investors may quietly surrender the engine of real compounding. Yet today's portfolio tools, like WisdomTree's capital-efficient strategies, offer a more sophisticated way forward: a chance to embrace the tactical advantages of gold without abandoning the structural advantages of stocks. In a market defined by new threats and new technologies, the smartest investors aren't making blunt trade-offs—they're finding ways to stack advantages. The future belongs to those who can hedge wisely without giving up their claim on growth.

Figure 8: Additional Information

Footnote

1 Refers to Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa.

Important Risks Related to this Article

Source for all data in this blog post unless otherwise noted: J.J. Siegel & J. Schwartz, (2022), Stocks for the long run: The definitive guide to financial market returns and long-term investment strategies (6th ed.), McGraw Hill.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: Ready For More Growth Despite Q1 Earnings Miss
data.description:
I have been covering ADMA Biologics (ADMA) since December of 2018, when I highlighted the company’s impressive profile of being a fully integrated biopharmaceutical company that was working hard to get BIVIGAM and ASCENIV approved, while also expanding their plasma collection network and manufacturing capacity. I was stressing that ADMA has the ingredients to report rapid growth in revenue, profitability, and operational scalability in only a few years. Yet, ADMA’s share price remained in the doldrums of the small-cap biotech arena, despite the company starting to show plenty of signs that they were executing their strategy and making headway on all fronts. The market was simply overlooking ADMA’s progress, and there is no better example than my “ADMA Biologics: Feeling Bullish After Encouraging Q2 Earnings Report” article from August of 2021. At that time, ADMA was already showing strong growth with a 129% increase year-over-year in revenues, while also reducing their net loss and improving their gross margins. As a result, I was “feeling extremely bullish about ADMA’s long-term upside and will continue accumulating shares on dips in the share price.” Yet, the share price was $1.67 per share… Fast forward to May of 2025, and the company is still posting impressive earnings growth, and the ticker is up ~1,314%.

Indeed, not everything was perfect for ADMA back in 2021, and there were certainly uncertainties that could have made investors apprehensive about driving the share price higher. However, for me, it seemed apparent that the company had the ingredients to succeed, and their Q1 2025 earnings were where they were able to post a rare combination of missing on both EPS and revenue… yet, they had strong top-line acceleration, margin expansion, and balance sheet strengthening.

In addition, the company raised their guidance, gaining FDA approval for their yield-enhancement process, starting a stock repurchase program, and initiating further pipeline potential. Although the company might have “missed” on earnings, I believe the bullish long-term outlook suggests the ticker might be ready to graduate from the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” portfolio to my “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

I intend to review the company’s Q1 earnings and will discuss the company’s updated guidance, increased yield, ASCENIV’s progress, pipeline updates, balance sheet status, and share repurchase program. In addition, I will point out some lingering risks that investors should consider when managing their ADMA position. Finally, I discuss my preparations for graduating ADMA to my “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

Q1 Review

ADMA Biologics posted a breakout performance for Q1 that led to a strong beat on EPS and revenue. ADMA logged $114.8M in revenue, which is up 40% year-over-year. The company’s flagship product, ASCENIV, is experiencing strong demand, which is causing their revenue mix to shift and improve margin expansion. The company's gross profit was $61.1M (53.2%), which is up from $39.1M (47.8%) year-over-year.

ADMA’s GAAP net income surged 51% year-over-year to $26.9M, while their adjusted net income increased by 87% to $33.3M. ADMA also revealed that their adjusted EBITDA was up 81% to $47.9M, suggesting strong operational and earnings leverage.

Collectively, ADMA’s earnings reveal that the company is accelerating across the board with high-margin, high-growth commercial operations that are leading to impressive profitability metrics.

Strengthening the Balance Sheet

Another point to highlight is the company’s financial health. ADMA ended Q1 with $171M in total cash and receivables. This cash position should give the company the liquidity they need to fund growth and reduce their debt. I will note that the company recently reorganized their debt structure by employing their revolving credit facility to repay all but $2.5M of the Ares Capital term loan. This action has reduced the company’s cost of debt by 1.1%.

The company is not done yet, with their EBITDA and cash flow increasing, management expects further deleveraging. Thus, giving them some more flexibility to consider some strategic acquisitions or improvements to promote growth.

More Yield, Bigger Guidance

Reading through the company’s Q1 earnings release, the most significant development was the FDA approval of their yield enhancement production process. Using a novel manufacturing advancement, ADMA is expected to boost their immunoglobulin yield by 20%, but is using the same volume of starting plasma. Not only will this be operationally accretive, but ADMA could be the leader in plasma yield optimization.

The company has yet to include this upgrade in their fiscal year 2025 guidance, but they have adjusted their preliminary 2026 revenue guidance to surpass $625M. ADMA currently has their 2025 guidance at over $500M, so a potential 25% revenue increase for 2026. What is more, their adjusted EBITDA for 2025 is now projected to rise above $235M, and to more than $340M in 2026. The company also reiterated that their floor-adjusted net income for 2025 is $175M, and they are raising their 2026 guidance to a minimum of $245M. Clearly, the company is expecting the remainder of 2025 and 2026 to be transformative.

Moreover, ADMA management even raised their pre-2030 annual revenue target to $1.1B or more. So, it looks as if ASCENIV’s increased adoption, their production efficiencies, and ASCENIV’s IP protection beyond 2035 have allowed management to promote a very bullish outlook for the remainder of this decade.

Pipeline Potential

I must point out that the company’s guidance adjustments do not include any potential contributions from any of their development pipeline programs. ADMA’s lead pipeline program, SG-001, is a hyperimmune globulin targeting Streptococcus pneumoniae. If successful, SG-001 could generate $300M-$500M per year. In addition, the company believes that its IP could last through at least 2037. So, ADMA might have another big growth opportunity pending.

ADMA expects to have SG-001’s preclinical proof-of-concept animal data by year-end.

Share Repurchase Program Revealed

ADMA also announced that they are starting a $500M share repurchase program thanks to their free cash flow generation and intrinsic value. ADMA management suggested that the company could use their buybacks to offset dilution or counteract ADMA’s undervaluation, which is a result of the biotech sector’s volatility.

Typically, I would be hesitant about a company the size of ADMA committing to a share repurchase program when they are still looking to fund some important growth initiatives. However, their adjusted net income is forecasted to rise above $200M in 2026, so the cash should be there.

I will also note that the fact the company is looking to start a share repurchase program at this stage of growth suggests that ADMA management is not bullish about the future… they are almost certain they will hit their publicized forecasts.

Lingering Risks

Despite my bullish outlook on ADMA, I must highlight several risks that investors should keep an eye out for. Primarily, the company is heavily reliant on the success of ASCENIV. Therefore, regulatory, safety, or reimbursement issues could impact ASCENIV’s commercial performance, which would likely have a dramatic impact on the company’s earnings until the issues are resolved.

I will reiterate longstanding risks that I have been highlighting for years. The company needs plasma to produce their commercial products. Although ADMA has long-term plasma agreements and their internal collection capabilities, these sources are dependent on donors. So, without a steady supply of donors, the raw material for the company’s IG products could be restricted and constrain the company’s production.

One of the more recent risks is that an extreme change in regulatory or healthcare policy shifts could impact reimbursement for biologics. It is possible that the new administration might be looking to make substantial changes in Medicare or Medicaid reimbursement rates for biologics, or introduce new healthcare legislation to replace the Affordable Care Act, which could create both cost and demand pressures, as well as reimbursement hurdles.

Despite these risks, I am maintaining my ADMA conviction level at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain a “Top Idea” in the Compounding Healthcare Seeking Alpha Marketplace Service.

Getting Ready to Graduate

Admittedly, I was a bit concerned that the company missed the Street’s estimates for both EPS and revenue. However, after reading through the company’s release and listening to the earnings call, I still see ADMA Biologics as one of the most impressive mid-cap growth stories over the past several years. It seems as if ADMA is their way to record revenues, swelling EBITDA and earnings growth, and a fortified balance sheet.

Looking at the Street’s estimates below, we can see that analysts are also quite bullish on ADMA Biologics with substantial forecasted growth for both EPS and revenue.

That being said, I don’t see any updates from Street analysts since the company announced their FDA approval of the yield enhancement process (9 Days Ahead of Q1 Earnings). So, it is likely those estimates get revised upwards in the coming quarters. Into the bargain, the company is already forecasting $1.1B in revenue for 2030. This step-function improvement in ASCENIV output, along with a high-margin pipeline candidate like SG-001, a large-scale share repurchases program, and an imposing IP portfolio, has forced me to start making preparations for ADMA to move from my “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio to the “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

The process of taking my "house money" ADMA position from my speculative Bio Boom portfolio to my Bioreactor growth portfolio, a change in mindset and mechanics. First, I will be re-underwriting ADMA with a focus on the company’s fundamentals, and not just their potential. The evaluation framework will be shifted from high-upside prospects to the company’s operational leverage, capital discipline, and most importantly, earnings durability. Looking back at ADMA’s recent earnings history, I believe they have established a respectable track record of revenue growth over 40%, along with margin expansion, and impressive profitability metrics, which are all checkboxes for graduation readiness.

Once ADMA is finally placed in the Bioreactor portfolio, I will immediately resize the position with a midweight allocation to adjust for the portfolio’s risk-reward profile. So, the position will be larger than what it was in the Bio Boom portfolio, but it would be smaller than the compounders that are in my “Healthy Dividend” portfolio.

As a result, I will be looking to add to my ADMA position over the remainder of 2025 to get it up to speed with my other Bioreactor positions by the end of 2026. However, I will have a probationary approach that will require ADMA to maintain 25–30% revenue growth, continued profitability, and expanding margins. If they deliver consistently over the next few quarters, I will consider increasing my allocation.

ADMA’s graduation to the Bioreactor portfolio does not change my overall goal of trading the ticker in order to generate profit, while still amassing a larger “house money” position to hold for a long-term investment.

data.title: Amazon's Operating Margins Continue To Improve
data.description:
Introduction

Per my February article, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) employees are getting more productive. The 1Q25 numbers have come out since then and there are positive developments including continued progress with seasonally adjusted operating margins.

My thesis is that Amazon’s operating margins continue to improve.

The Numbers

Per comments from CFO Brian Olsavsky in the 1Q25 call, there were one-time charges for the period impacting the North America and international segments. These charges hurt operating margins for the segments by 90 basis points and 70 basis points, respectively. These charges were related to historical returns and costs to receive inventory which was pulled forward because of expected tariffs.

North American operating margin for the 1Q25 period was 6.3%, and it would have been 7.2% were it not for the one-time charges mentioned above. For comparison, North American operating margin for 1Q24 was 5.8%. International operating margin for 1Q25 was 3%, and it would have been 3.7% were it not for the one-time charges. Looking at the 1Q24 period from a year ago, operating margin for international was 2.8%.

CFO Olsavsky went on in the 1Q25 call to explain factors leading to improved margins (emphasis added):

In Q1, our newly re-architected inbound network drove productivity in our fulfillment and transportation network, leading to better inventory placement and higher units per package, and as a result, lower delivery costs. Beyond Q1, we have a number of initiatives underway to continue improving our cost structure, such as fine-tuning our inbound network, building out our same-day delivery sites, expanding our rural delivery network, and adding robotics and automation to our facilities. Better inventory placement remains a top priority. Better placement drives more in-stock selection, reduces travel distances, and speeds up delivery. And having inventory in the right place at the right time increases the likelihood that multiple items can be combined in a package, which helps reduce packaging and cost. Although progress won't always be linear, we have a good plan to continue to drive improvement over time.

The above comments about lowering delivery costs are important moving forward because fulfillment increased more than sales from 1Q24 to 1Q25. In other words, the Y/Y change for fulfillment was 10.2% from 1Q24 to 1Q25 while the Y/Y change for sales was only 8.6% ($ in millions):

I think this can continue to become a smaller percentage of sales as Amazon keeps fine-tuning their inbound network. It is stressful seeing the technology and infrastructure increase at a higher rate than net sales, but much of this is for AWS where the Y/Y sales growth is higher than the overall Y/Y sales growth. Again, the above improvements are even more impressive when we take into account the comments from the 1Q25 call which revealed 1Q25 margins for North America and international would have been 7.2% and 3.7%, respectively, were it not for one-time charges.

Valuation

The increases we’re seeing in capex are concerning and there is risk here. If management miscalculates such that the depreciation and amortization are understated, then accrual earnings are no longer a good proxy for the amount of cash which can be pulled out of the business. And of course, a company is worth the amount of cash we can pull out of it from now until judgment day. In 1Q24, the depreciation and amortization of $11,684 million was 78% of the gross capex of $14,925 million at the time. Things are different now. In 1Q25, the depreciation and amortization of $14,262 million is only 57% of the gross capex of $25,019 million. I believe CEO Andy Jassy when he says the AI capex investments are the opportunity of a lifetime, but part of me still has concerns.

Another valuation risk concern is the slowing of the non-AWS growth rate. Historically, sales for the first quarter is always lower than the preceding seasonal fourth quarter, but the first quarter numbers have held up nicely against the previous third quarter numbers until now. This is easy to see in a graph where the 1P and 3P volume numbers for 3Q24 are greater than they were for 1Q25:

$111,786

$71,691

$113,903

$650,313

We need to think about overall EPS growth, its drivers, cash flows and new factors such as tariffs. As we said above, there are concerns with some non-AWS considerations such as 1P and 3P sales growth. If we take out the seasonal fourth quarter, we see these numbers shrank instead of growing from 3Q24 to 1Q25. Additionally, these segments only grew 5% and 6%, respectively, Y/Y. The advertising business is also non-AWS, and it grew nicely at 18% Y/Y, but it also struggled on a quarterly basis, falling from 3Q24 to 1Q25. Still, from an overall perspective, AWS is more important for EPS growth and AWS did well in terms of growth both Y/Y and Q/Q (no seasonality adjustment is needed here). I believe AWS will continue to drive overall EPS growth in the quarters and years ahead. Again, the obvious concern here is the mismatch between cash flow and accrual earnings as AWS continues to become a more important part of accrual earnings. Tariffs can impact both retail and AWS. On the retail side, I’m optimistic 3P sellers can make adjustments to keep volume moving. On the AWS side, I think demand will remain strong for compute all over the world despite the changing landscape we’re seeing with tariffs.

In my last article, I was optimistic non-AWS operating margins could eventually reach 7%, but now I feel even better about the prospects and I think they could one day be 8%. Trailing twelve months (“TTM”) operating income for non-AWS segments is $538,527 million. If we use a hypothetical 8% margin, then the hypothetical operating earning power is $43 billion. In the past, I’ve used a 35 to 40x multiple on this, but now I think that might be a bit ambitious. If we use a multiple of 30 to 35x instead, then it gives us a non-AWS valuation range of $1,290 to $1,505 billion.

AWS has 1Q25 operating income of $11,547 million, which gives us a run rate of $46.2 billion. The segment continues to grow nicely, and it benefits from being the first hyperscaler such that it has a higher margin than competitors like Google Cloud even when sales are similar. In other words, Google Cloud had sales of $12.3 billion in 1Q25 and their operating margin was 17.5%, but AWS had a margin of 28.3% back in the 4Q20 period when their sales were $12.7 billion:

In the past, I’ve used a multiple of 25 to 30x for AWS and this is lower than the multiple for non-AWS, in part because I worry about the heavy capex requirements for AWS. Applying the multiple gives us a valuation range of $1,155 to $1,385 billion when rounding to the nearest $5 billion. I’m trying to be conservative and if the depreciation figures are accurate, then AWS probably has a higher valuation range than what I’ve shown here.

Summing up, my valuation range is as follows:

$1,290 to $1,505 billion non-AWS

$1,155 to $1,385 AWS

—-----------------------------------------

$2,445 to $2,890 billion

The 1Q25 10-Q shows 10,616,352,407 shares as of April 23. Multiplying by the May 7 share price of $188.71 gives us a market cap of $2 trillion. The market cap is below my valuation range and I think the stock is a buy for long-term investors who will hold it for at least three years.

Forward-looking investors should monitor the capex spend in upcoming quarters and scrutinize assumptions with depreciation and amortization.

data.title: The U.K. Is Relying On EU Trade Reset To Bolster Its Public Finances
data.description:
By James Smith

The UK is seeking to secure a number of deals

Trade deals are like buses. You don’t see one for weeks, then two – maybe even three – come along at once.

The UK and India have, after years of talks, agreed a deal that will reduce tariffs for some UK-made goods in exchange for tax concessions on Indian workers moving to Britain, amongst other things. And if reports are accurate, the UK has virtually reached a deal with the US on exempting at least a portion of British steel and car exports from President Trump’s sectoral tariffs. Time will tell whether that gets signed.

The UK is walking a fine line on both sets of negotiations, cautious not to give concessions that could jeopardise talks with the European Union, which could prove much more consequential.

This is not simply about reducing reliance on the US. All of these negotiations, particularly those with the EU, are designed above all to ease the pressure on Britain’s public finances.

The logic is simple. The Office for Budget Responsibility, which polices the fiscal rules, judged that Brexit would generate a permanent 4% hit to productivity. Lower productivity means slower economic growth. And slower economic growth makes it harder to reduce budget deficits and stabilise debt-to-GDP ratios, which is what the UK’s fiscal rules mandate.

UK trade with the EU is more significant than with the US

Closer UK-EU ties could be helpful for the public finances

Closer EU trades will, the UK government hopes, convince the OBR that some of this productivity hit can be undone.

Formally, the UK has said it doesn’t want to regain single market or customs union membership. But those red lines could get significantly watered down over the coming months.

The government has said it wants to agree to a veterinary agreement, which would see Britain realign with EU food standards and remove cumbersome border checks. In practice, we think that an agreement could cover a much wider range of goods exports. The UK hasn’t meaningfully diverged from EU product regulation since Brexit, after all.

Of course, the EU would have to agree, and for some in Brussels, this probably sounds a lot like cherry-picking. Then again, the political climate has changed dramatically since the UK left the EU in 2020. Migration isn’t the priority it once was, while the UK has a strong hand to play on defence.

Both sides meet for a summit on 19 May, and positive headlines could potentially have a tangible impact on UK markets, even if those initial talks are centred on military rather than economic matters.

Whether this is a game-changer for the UK’s public finances, we’re not so sure. That 4% productivity hit to the OBR's forecasts is not going to be fully regained, given the UK isn't going to completely re-align with the EU. And that limits the scope for upward revisions to those OBR growth projections, even if a deal can be reached before the Autumn budget.

In fact, given the more sombre global economic outlook, it’s more likely those forecasts will get revised down. And that makes further tax rises look increasingly unavoidable later this year.

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Lower U.S. Consumer Spending Can Already Be Seen
data.description:
As of March 2025, a meaningful slice of US consumers reported spending less at retail stores and online compared to last year. Some Americans are cutting back their restaurant dining, cinema attendance and even online video subscriptions.

The S&P Global Market Intelligence Kagan first quarter 2025 Consumer Insights survey asked respondents to assess how their behavior in 2025 has compared to last year. For instance, 29% of total internet adults said they were spending less this year at retail stores, compared to only 12% citing increased year-over-year spending.

With regard to online purchases, 23% said they were spending less in 2025, while 20% reported a higher level of spending this year. Over one-third (36%) indicated they were dining out at restaurants less frequently this year, more than double the 14% that have increased their restaurant visits.

Among those spending more at retail stores in 2025 than last year, nearly half (47%) were young adults, ages 18-34. Older adults, aged 55 and above constitute approximately one-quarter (24%) of those with increased retail spending this year. Those spending less at retail stores in 2025 were slightly skewed toward older 55+ adults (41%) with 32% in the 18-34 age bracket.

The 2025 age distribution among those spending more or less from online sources appears to be more uniform than for retail store spending. Approximately four in 10 (39%) of those indicating increased online spending this year were older 55+ adults; very similar to the 36% of younger 18-34 year-olds who expected higher online spending in 2025. The age distribution for those spending less online in 2025 closely mirrored that of the increased online spenders.

When it comes to dining out at restaurants, the survey data shows that older Americans tend to be cutting back, while younger adults are eating out more frequently. The data reveals that 43% of those who said they are spending less at restaurants in 2025 were adults aged 55 and older. Similarly, 43% of those with increased restaurant visits year over year were young adults (18-34).

When US consumers cut their retail store and online spending, which retailers will continue to get their money? To assess this, the following chart depicts the percentage point variance between the retailers used by those with reduced spending in 2025 and the overall survey.

For example, the survey found that 32% of those with reduced 2025 spending shop at the Home Depot (HD), compared to 37% of the total survey sample, yielding a negative five percentage point variance. Reduced consumer spending has the potential to impact nearly all retailers, ranging from e-commerce giants like Amazon (AMZN) to big box retailers (Target (TGT) and Costco (COST)), drug stores and the home improvement sector.

The entertainment sector is not immune to economic pressures that reduce consumer spending. The survey found that 27% of US internet adults have fewer online video subscriptions (SVOD) in 2025 than last year, while 16% have subscribed to additional SVOD services.

Over one-third (35%) said they have been attending the cinema less in 2025, more than three times (11%) those that are seeing more movies this year. Time spent watching free online video appears to be fairly stable with 21% having more viewing hours in 2025, compared to 18% with fewer viewing hours.

Among those spending less at retail stores or online in 2025, 58% said they were also attending the cinema less often, and approximately half (47%) have fewer SVOD subscriptions than last year.

Yet, 36% of these reduced spenders said they also are spending less time watching free online video, significantly more than the 26% that reported having more free online video viewing hours this year. This suggests that free online video services will not necessarily benefit greatly from consumers spending less on SVOD services and in-theater movies.

data.title: METU Does What It Says On The Tin, 200% META Stock
data.description:
Introduction

It's been easy to be a Meta Platforms (META) bull, so much so that it was included in both the FANG and Mag 7 groupings of stocks, and has been on a multi-year run that has outperformed the broader Mag 7 group and the S&P 500 over the last few years, by significant margins.

This has brought a lot of attention to META, and has been encouraging for investors interested in accessing leverage for META stock.

After my review of PLTU, a fund that provides leverage on Palantir (PLTR) stock, I began to look through the rest of fund manager Direxion's catalog. Immediately, I wanted to review the Direxion Daily META Bull 2X Shares (NASDAQ:METU), which aims to provide, according to the Direxion website:

...daily investment results, before fees and expenses, of 200%...of the performance of the common shares of Meta Platforms, Inc.

Before we proceed any further with this METU review, any time I introduce leveraged investments, it's important to cover the major risks involved with these kinds of assets.

Warning: Major Risks Ahead

There are three warnings that I think investors should consider when looking at any daily-resetting leveraged fund:

1) The SEC notes:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that the performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

2) The fund's prospectus notes:

The Funds seek daily leveraged investment results and are intended to be used as short-term trading vehicles. Each Fund attempts to provide daily investment results that correspond to 2 times the performance of an underlying security. The Funds are not intended to be used by, and are not appropriate for, investors who do not intend to actively monitor and manage their portfolios...

The Funds are not suitable for all investors. The Funds are designed to be utilized only by sophisticated investors, such as traders and active investors employing dynamic strategies...

If a Fund's underlying security moves more than 50% on a given trading day in a direction adverse to the Fund, the Fund's investors would lose all of their money.

3) This review of MSTX, the "Case Study For Bad Leveraged ETFs" as a cautionary tale.

META vs. METU

The way that these funds operate is that they reset daily, meaning every day, investors should get 2x the performance of META stock on that day. Because it is not long-term leverage, this means that moves are outsized in both directions, since METU also falls 2x the amount META down on bad days.

Despite at one point being up over 80% from its inception, METU is now underperforming META as a long-term holding. This is what makes it not suitable for investors to buy and hold. It went from being up over 80% to being down more than 20% in a not-so-short amount of time.

These swings can be too much for the psychology of many investors, even if they have a plan to use METU as a non-day trading tool. Consider also the warning in the prospectus about the 50% decline in META meaning certain destruction for the fund.

While this hasn't happened in META's history, it does hold the record for the last one-day wipe out of wealth in a single stock, at a whopping $251B shaved off the market cap that day. It was a 22% drop by the end, and would have cut METU nearly in half had it been around back then.

Those kinds of quick and sudden drops are devastating for these kinds of leveraged funds like METU, and the reason investors may not be better off holding them, or even trading them, than the actual stock. We can take a look at the most recent fall on April 4th, which METU was around for, to see how negatively sudden drops (and it wasn't META's fault this time!) can really erode investor gains.

The loss was so sharp that even when META spiked the following week and recovered, METU was still at a loss. Subsequent slow, grinding lower days on META following the recovery tanked METU far below the April 4th crash.

METU Holdings & Strategy

The actual holdings in the fund are fairly straightforward. Here is the full holdings list for METU:

There are several components that we can see: the cash component, total return swaps on META stock, and META stock itself, which is to ensure that the fund doesn't go to zero in a 50% drop. That 10% allocation to META stock will buoy the fund, and it should only lose 90% of its NAV.

The fund's prospectus notes this as part of the adviser's plan to ensure there can't be a full one-day wipe out of the fund, although I would personally consider losing 90% of my investment a wipe out.

The Funds' investment adviser...Will attempt to position each Fund's portfolio to ensure that a Fund does not gain or lose more than 90% of its net asset value on a given trading day. As a consequence, a Fund's portfolio should not be responsive to underlying security movements beyond 45% on a given trading day, whether that movement is favorable or adverse to the Fund.

On the flip side, that means the fund won't gain linearly on a day when META jumps over 45%, not that that has ever happened, and likely never will. META's largest daily gain on record was back in February 2024, which is one of the largest daily gains of all time, and still only comes in at 20%.

Suitability

I am a believer that leveraged ETFs can be useful tools, but not great long-term investments. This is especially true for those that reset daily, and for those that focus on a single stock like METU does.

META on its own can be very volatile; in this article, we discussed that it held records (still does for losses, but is second to NVDA for gains) for intraday volatility. Many investors may find that META on its own is already pushing their risk tolerance, let alone a leveraged position.

However, there is a use case for METU speculating on events like earnings reports where META could run up, making METU sprint.

Employ heavy caution when making these trades, monitor positions closely, and consider allocating no more than 2% of a speculative portfolio to this fund at the most; single stock leverage is not to be trifled with. It should also be noted that investors should avoid holding this fund overnight, as the risk of a catalyst making the stock move dramatically during a time when you cannot trade could result in horrific losses (or gains, depending on the catalyst).

Conclusion

The Direxion Daily META Bull 2X Shares is a fund made for trading, but that's not inherently a bad thing. For what it is, Direxion does a great job, and the fund seems to track Meta Platforms stock very well, offering daily 2x leverage with few errors that appeared in its short lifetime.

It's important for traders to note the tools at our disposal, and which ones are useful. Some of them are not, and most leveraged funds I review, I am not impressed with. METU is one exception, as its construction and performance show that the fund is being run with prudence and risk management in mind (at least, in the scope of leveraged ETFs), with the managers building in support to ensure the fund cannot be wiped out in a single day.

Engage with this fund at your own peril, but know that it should achieve its desired results, 200% performance of META on a given day, up to a ±45% move in META, based on the data we have for it.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Verisk Analytics: Rating Downgrade As Valuation Multiple Climbs
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Verisk Analytics (NASDAQ:VRSK) was a buy rating because I believed the strong growth outlook could support the valuation premium, and the upside was attractive. The solid 1Q25 results validated my thesis, which helps me to stay convinced in the business's fundamentals. However, with the significant run-up in valuation to ~43x forward PE, the risk/reward profile has shifted. The current multiple limits near-term upside, and hence, I am downgrading to a hold rating.

1Q25 results update: Subscription revenue continues to be the main driver

VRSK 1Q25 was a solid start to FY25. Total revenue grew 7.9% on an organic basis to $753 million, which was a 100 bps acceleration vs. 1Q24, suggesting that underlying demand is still healthy. This growth performance was also ahead of my FY25 assumption of 7% growth. What was really encouraging regarding the topline performance is the strength in subscription revenue. If you recall my previous writeup, this has been a big part of my bull case, and 1Q25 results showed that momentum isn't slowing down. Per the earnings release, 83% of insurance revenue now comes from hosted subscriptions-up from 80% in 1Q24. That mix shift matters a lot because it makes the business model more stable and predictable. Growth was solid too: subscription revenue grew 10.6% y/y on an organic basis.

Margins also did well. Gross margin expanded by 170 bps y/y, which drove adj. EBITDA margin up to 55.3% (130 bps y/y expansion). While adj. net came down a little, this was primarily due to the difference in tax rate. Adj. EPS still managed to grow ~7%, up to $1.73 for 1Q25. More importantly, management is sticking with its FY25 outlook: revenue of $3.03-3.08 billion, adj. EBITDA of $1.67-1.72 billion (implying 55-56% margins), and EPS of $6.80-7.10. This is good, especially in what I would say is a very uncertain macro backdrop.

Value-Based Pricing and GTM Enhancements Bearing Fruit

In my last write-up, I talked about VRSK pivoting to value-based pricing and its new go-to-market [GTM] strategy as key levers for growth. In the 1Q25 results, VRSK has proven that these initiatives are working. Management clearly pointed out strong price realization as a driver behind the subscription growth, which to me is a clear sign that the value-based pricing approach is working. This also tells us that VRSK is increasingly moving away from having its revenue growth being purely tied to client premium volumes.

The GTM restructuring I highlighted previously is turning out to be the right move as I expected, and I inferred this from management's commentary around better engagement:

"Continued to enhance client engagement through our refined go-to-market strategy, resulting in deeper relationships and a better understanding of client needs across the enterprise…" 1Q25 press release

I have touched briefly on the positive impacts of this GTM change, but I now think a strong flywheel effect may start to kick in. Here is how I see it. A better alignment (the sales team having the right knowledge and incentives) drives deeper customer conversations, and this leads to stronger pricing power (offering the right product) and stickier relationships (more products adopted means VRSK is more embedded in the workflow), and this gives VRSK more financial capacity to reinvest into better GTM alignment.

Product Innovation and Acquisition of Simplitium

Finally, regarding the last growth lever I mentioned before-product innovation-it is also working very well. And the part that convinces me is that it is driving P&L performance. Although we do not know the direct contribution, it was noted that underwriting revenue growth was driven by model and content enhancements (source: VRSK 10-Q). What reinforces my bullish view is that VRSK is enhancing its proprietary data. As I have said before, having the right data is key to enabling better decision-making. VRSK's April acquisition of Simplitium does this perfectly. This is more than just a model-count improvement; I think it is a smart, strategic move to expand the VRSK risk platform as it layers on >300 third-party models. This significantly broadens the perspectives that clients can view risk, especially for clients with specialized or emerging risk needs (i.e., beyond the scope of what VRSK originally has).

This acquisition also supports VRSK expansion plans in Europe, and I think the implications here are more significant than the headline suggests. By acquiring Simplitium, VRSK now has a faster time-to-market, be it through an existing customer base or through access to models that are already tailored for European regulatory frameworks and risk categories. That makes the VRSK platform immediately more relevant to local insurers. On top of that, it also equips VRSK with local expertise. I think this really deserves a highlight, as local market knowledge and relationships matter a lot when you're trying to scale in a new market.

Valuation

The only problem here is that VRSK valuation has run a lot higher than I expected. While the share price has yet to meet my original target price of $324, valuation has gone right up to ~43x forward PE. I don't think VRSK is worth >40x given its growth outlook. For comparison, similar peers in the data solution industry like S&P Global, MSCI, and Moody's Corp. only trade in the ~30x forward PE range. My view is that because of the potential for growth acceleration (as all the growth levers continue to get pulled, which VRSK is executing well), it should trade higher than its historical average (my base cash model has it at +1 standard deviation-36x).

Keeping my assumptions the same-acceleration to 12% revenue growth and modest margin expansion to 34% by FY27, and multiples rerate to 36x-I see an 8% upside from here. As much as I like to think the bull case can hold (multiples stay at >40x), the base case upside is not as attractive anymore. There is also a risk that multiples can get contracted fast in the near term if growth disappoints. As such, I downgraded to a hold rating.

Conclusion

My view for VRSK is now a hold rating. While the business continues to execute well across its strategic initiatives, the current valuation appears to fully reflect these positives. The limited upside in my base case, coupled with the risk of multiple contractions should growth expectations moderate, suggests that staying on the sidelines is a better move today.
